verses.text: Eliphaz asks whether man can profit God, or whether Job's righteousness brings the Almighty any gain. The point, that God needs nothing from us, is true. But he twists it to suggest God has no real interest in vindicating the upright.

Eliphaz asks whether man can profit God, or whether Job's righteousness brings the Almighty any gain. The point, that God needs nothing from us, is true. But he twists it to suggest God has no real interest in vindicating the upright.

Now he charges Job outright with great wickedness — stripping the poor of pledges, denying bread and water, sending widows away empty. The accusations are invented. Desperate to save his theory, Eliphaz slanders an innocent man with sins he never committed.

Now he charges Job outright with great wickedness — stripping the poor of pledges, denying bread and water, sending widows away empty. The accusations are invented. Desperate to save his theory, Eliphaz slanders an innocent man with sins he never committed.

Therefore, he says, snares surround Job and sudden darkness covers him. He reads Job's trouble as the wage of these supposed crimes. To assign imagined causes to real suffering is to bear false witness.

Therefore, he says, snares surround Job and sudden darkness covers him. He reads Job's trouble as the wage of these supposed crimes. To assign imagined causes to real suffering is to bear false witness.

Is not God high in heaven, Eliphaz asks, yet Job supposedly says, 'how doth God know?' as if clouds hide us from Him. He paints Job as a secret unbeliever. It is a grave wrong to put godless words into a believer's mouth.

Is not God high in heaven, Eliphaz asks, yet Job supposedly says, 'how doth God know?' as if clouds hide us from Him. He paints Job as a secret unbeliever. It is a grave wrong to put godless words into a believer's mouth.

He warns Job not to walk the old way of the wicked, swept off before their time, though God had filled their houses with good. He recalls the flood-generation's doom. The history is true, but it is no portrait of Job.

The righteous, he says, see the ruin of the wicked and rejoice that their own substance stands. He pictures the godly gloating over judgment. Yet to delight in another's downfall is far from the spirit God commends.

The righteous, he says, see the ruin of the wicked and rejoice that their own substance stands. He pictures the godly gloating over judgment. Yet to delight in another's downfall is far from the spirit God commends.

Then comes real gold: 'acquaint now thyself with him, and be at peace,' receive His word, return to the Almighty, and lay up His treasure. The counsel is excellent in itself. Even a mistaken friend may utter truth that the humble heart can keep.

He promises that then Job will delight in God, lift up his face, pray and be heard, for 'he shall save the humble person.' The promise is sweet and largely true. God does indeed lift the lowly, though not on the terms Eliphaz lays down.
